Tafseer:
The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was addressed by a word that brings before us a picture reflecting the internal state that mostly enveloped him during the period of this Surah's revelation.
'Muzzammil' is originally 'mutazzammil'. According to the rules of Arabic grammar, the letter 'ta' has been assimilated into the letter 'za'. A similar process occurred in the word 'muddaththir'. Its meaning, as noted previously, is 'the one who wraps himself in a garment'. This state is generally of a person who is anxious about the prevailing circumstances and disheartened by the attitude of the people around him. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was warning people of a punishment hovering over their heads, but the people's indifference and aversion were such that, let alone listening to his words, they would rush to attack him and describe his anxiety and compassion as madness and insanity. Under such circumstances, his being anxious and grieved was natural, and in a state of anxiety and grief, a person's garment, as noted, is his best companion. Wrapping himself in it, he can disconnect from creation and connect with the Creator whenever he wills. It should be noted that a garment (chador) was a highly important part of the Arabs' dress, and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) used to keep a garment with him.
Narrations state that even before the mission of prophethood, when he was searching for the truth, he would often experience such states of isolation until Allah Almighty, as indicated in Surah Ad-Duha, guided him. Then, this state enveloped him with greater intensity when he personally experienced his diseased nation's aversion to the medicine and hostility toward the physician. The state that enveloped him because of this experience is what the word 'Muzzammil' points to.
Clearing a misunderstanding: Some commentators have interpreted this address to mean that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was sleeping wrapped in a garment when he was instructed through revelation: 'O you who are sleeping wrapped in a garment, rise and pray.' Although this meaning is interesting because it apparently aligns with the subsequent verses, in our view, it is incorrect. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was never among those who slept a sleep of heedlessness wrapped in a garment during any phase of his blessed life. He always slept a light sleep, and like his day, his night was also spent mostly in remembrance and reflection. Not a single verse of the Qur'an indicates that he was ever rebuked for heedlessness toward God; on the contrary, he was repeatedly corrected in a highly affectionate manner for carrying a heavier burden than what Allah Almighty had placed on him. However, it is found in various places of the Qur'an that when he faced difficulties and trials in the path of his call and grew anxious, he was urged to perform prayer—especially the night prayer (Tahajjud)—to strengthen his resolve and remove his anxiety. In our view, the same is the case here.

The blessings of the night prayer:
The anxiety and distress implied in the word 'Muzzammil' is being addressed with a remedy, and this remedy has been prescribed for him throughout the Qur'an whenever he faced difficulties in the path of his call. We have explained its wisdom in various places. Here, since the impact and value of this remedy are explained further on, instead of adding our own explanation, we will restrict ourselves to the commentary of the verses, which will make it clear what blessings Allah Almighty has placed in the night prayer for a believer, and in what aspects it serves to restore his exhausted energy and multiply his resolve and faith.
The time of the night prayer: 'Arise [to pray] the night, except for a little—its half or subtract from it a little or add to it' (qumi-l-layla illa qaleela, nisfahu awi-nqush minhu qaleela, aw zid 'alayh) - The night here obviously refers to the latter half of the night, when a person rises after having slept for a while. This is clarified further by the word 'nashi'at-al-layl'. This time for Tahajjud is established from the practice of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and is also the most blessed time for the primary objective. This duration can be equivalent to half the night, slightly less than that, or more. Rising during the latter part of the night is a difficult task, and there is a possibility of minor delays, which is why flexibility is kept regarding the time so that it does not cause undue hardship. Although the literal text suggests that the full half of the night is preferred, the flexibility for minor variations is present in the words.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 2, 3, and 4 was combined in the original text.]

The manner of reciting the Qur'an:
'And recite the Qur'an with measured recitation' (wa rattili-l-qur'ana tarteela) - This states the manner of reciting the Qur'an: recite it in prayer in a measured, distinct, and slow manner. Narrations state that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) recited the Qur'an with a beautiful, distinct tone, pausing at the end of each verse, occasionally repeating a single verse due to intense emotion, praying for refuge when reciting verses of wrath, and offering gratitude when reciting verses of mercy. Some verses containing a command or indication of prostration would prompt him to immediately fall in prostration.
This manner of recitation is in accordance with the direction of Allah Almighty and is narrated from the practice of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). From the perspective of the Qur'an's objective, only this manner can be beneficial. However, this manner remained among the Muslims only as long as they regarded the Qur'an as a source of reflection and a book of guidance for life. Later, when the Qur'an became merely a means for attaining reward and reciting for the deceased, it began to be recited in the rapid manner displayed by our reciters (huffadh) during Taraweeh and other recitations.

The meaning of 'a heavy word':
This points to the grand objective for which the night prayer was commanded. It is stated: We are soon going to place upon you a heavy word. This reveals that as a preparatory exercise and training for bearing this heavy word, you were commanded with this night prayer. What is meant by this heavy word? Commentators have narrated various opinions, but since they are not based on any solid proof, there is no benefit in reproducing them. Our teacher preferred the view that it refers to the general warning commanded in the next Surah:
(O you who covers himself, arise and warn) (Al-Muddaththir, 74:1-2)
and the subsequent verses. In our view, this opinion is highly reasonable. This is because from this general warning emerged the stages of disassociation, migration, and war, which represent the most difficult phases of the Prophet's life, during which he and his companions passed through such trials that make the heart tremble.
The distinguishing characteristic of striving to establish the religion: This reveals that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was commanded with the night prayer to prepare for the great struggle of establishing the religion that lay ahead of him and his companions. This distinguishing characteristic is what separates the struggle of establishing the religion from other movements. Before arranging other resources, what is required is a correct gnosis of the Lord, stable faith, and the night prayer—provided it is performed in the manner commanded. The foundation of the struggle to establish the religion lies on this rock. Without this foundation, if an attempt is made to construct the building of religion, it will collapse before it is even erected.

The wisdom of the night prayer:
This explains the wisdom behind commanding you with the night prayer. It is stated: because this time is highly suitable for the focus of the heart and mind, and most conducive for understanding the Qur'an.
'Nashi'ah' is derived from 'nasha'a' (to rise), serving as a verbal noun (masdar) on the pattern of 'aqibah' and 'afiyah'. 'Nashi'at-al-layl' refers to rising at night or night vigil. This word itself shows that the time for Tahajjud is indeed after sleeping for a while—namely, in the latter part of the night. Although rising at this time is difficult because sleep at this hour is highly pleasing, if a person passes this test, there is no time more blessed for remembering Allah and understanding His Book. One whom Allah Almighty blesses with the guidance to rise at this hour receives such a power to overcome his desires that it opens countless doors of self-reform. Furthermore, Allah Almighty, Who created the night and day, has reserved this time for the descent of His mercies, the doors of which open for His servants who recognize its value and present themselves as beggars at His door.
The impact of the night vigil: 'is more striking' (hiya ashaddu wat'an) - This states the impact of rising at this hour: when a person rises from his bed, performs wudu, and stands in prayer, his steps stand firm. Firm steps are a metaphor for the focus of the mind, the peace of the heart, and the alertness of the intellect. If the mind is distracted and the heart is restless, a person's steps do not stand firm, and he cannot perform even a small task with focus. Thus, their inner state is reflected through their outer posture. Some commentators have interpreted this to mean that rising at this hour is highly effective in crushing the desires of the soul. Although the word can accommodate this meaning, it does not align well with the subsequent phrase. The meaning I have adopted is also preferred by other commentators, but they did not point to the reality that firm steps are a metaphor for mental and spiritual focus.
'and more effective in speech' (wa aqwamu qeela) - That is: because this time is specifically for the focus of the mind and the alertness of the heart, whatever is uttered by the tongue is highly impactful and goes straight to the heart. The person himself accepts it as the testimony of his heart, and its impact on other listeners is unerring. The incident of the jinn described in Surah Al-Jinn is established from both narrations and context to have occurred during Tahajjud when they heard the Prophet reciting the Qur'an, and were so affected that they believed in it and arose as callers within their nation. The heart of a great person like Umar was also won by a similar event. This shows that reciting the Qur'an in Tahajjud in accordance with the prescribed manner serves not only to refine and purify one's own soul, but occasionally acts as a divine call to revive the souls and hearts of others, whether they are jinn or humans.

The interpretation of 'sabhan taweela':
Generally, people have interpreted this to mean that during the day, you have other tasks—meaning that since you are occupied with other duties during the day, it is difficult to find a quiet time for prayer; therefore, you were commanded with Tahajjud at night.
The word 'sabh' can accommodate this meaning from the perspective of lexicography, but my mind is not satisfied with this interpretation for several reasons:
First, the latter part of the night was chosen by Allah Almighty for the night prayer not merely because a person is occupied with other tasks during the day, but because the Qur'an indicates that this time, in its inherent qualities, is the most conducive for the objectives of the night prayer.
Second, if the intention was to say this, the simple style of expression would have been: 'Indeed, during the day you are occupied with many tasks' (inna laka fi-n-nahari shaghalan katheera) or other similar words. The word 'sabh' means swimming or moving about, and is not commonly used for being occupied with tasks unless supported by an explicit context, which is not present here.
Third, if the word 'sabh' carried this meaning here, its attribute should have been 'katheera' (many) or 'kabeera' (great) instead of 'taweela' (long).
For these reasons, the mind is not satisfied with that interpretation. In our view, 'sabh' (or taspbih) is used here in its recognized meaning of glorification, and the verse is interpreted as: besides the night prayer you are commanded with, you also have ample opportunity for glorification during the day, which you should observe. Indeed, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) observed the remembrance of Allah Almighty at all times—while rising, sitting, walking, eating, drinking, and sleeping. This is why supplications are narrated from him for every minor and major action. If a person observes them, his every step is accompanied by remembrance, and through their blessing, his worldly tasks are transformed into acts of worship.
What is demanded in religion is constant remembrance: The reality should be kept in mind that what is demanded in religion is constant remembrance. This was discussed in its relevant place. Just as breathing is necessary for physical life, remembering Allah is necessary for spiritual life. If breathing stops, the body dies; similarly, if heedlessness occurs, the soul perishes. The heart lives through constant remembrance, and the life of the heart is the true life.
Reflecting on the signs of the universe is also remembrance: Keep in mind the reality explained in various places—namely, that delivering the call of religion and reflecting on the signs of the universe are also included in remembrance. Indeed, reflecting is regarded as the superior remembrance because it is through this reflection that true meaning is generated within remembrance; without it, remembrance remains merely a verbal exercise, without any useful impact on life. This was discussed under: (Those who remember Allah standing, sitting, and [lying] on their sides and reflect on the creation of the heavens and the earth, [saying], 'Our Lord, You did not create this in vain; exalted are You, so protect us from the punishment of the Fire') (Al-Imran, 3:191).

The way to seek refuge in the Lord's mercy:
'Tabattul' and 'tabteel' mean disconnecting from creation and connecting with the Creator. This is the method Allah Almighty taught you: whenever you feel grieved by the people's disregard and harm, disconnect from these unworthy individuals and seek refuge in the mercy of your Lord, and for this, you need nothing other than remembering His name. When you remember Him by His name, He will protect you within His refuge.
Constantly keeping His attributes in mind: It should noted that all the names of Allah Almighty are expressions of His attributes, and all religion, Shariah, and faith are built upon His attributes. If the correct knowledge of His attributes is kept in mind, a person is protected by such a great host that all the armies of Satan appear like worthless straw to him. He feels himself more stable than mountains. But if the correct memory of His attributes is lost or weakened, his faith becomes weak, causing him—like the hypocrites—to assume that every lightning bolt is destined to strike his own field.

That is, the refuge of Allah is not the refuge of a weak being, but of the Lord of the east and the west. There is no deity besides Him who can associate with Him or hinder His decrees. If you take Him as a trustee, He is sufficient for you: (And sufficient is Allah as a trustee) (An-Nisa, 4:81).

Patience against the words of the opponents and its manner:
Be patient against the baseless words of your deniers and stand firm on your stance; neither grieve over their words nor follow them too much, but leave them with a beautiful separation. They themselves will face the consequence of their way.
Separating can happen in two ways. One is accompanied by public humiliation, insults, and feelings of enmity and revenge, which is the way of common worldly people. The righteous do not adopt this method; they strive for the reform of creation not for any personal benefit, but for guidance and the pleasure of Allah. When people disregard them and cause them pain, they feel sorrow and regret over their destiny rather than anger or hatred. Under such circumstances, they are forced to separate from them, but this separation is like that of a noble father who separates from his unruly son in silence. This kind of separation is described here as 'hajr jameel' (beautiful separation). This separation occasionally yields useful results: those who possess any spark of goodness are affected by this noble behavior and review their attitude; or at least, they realize that this person is not going to approve of their falsehood, no matter what happens. As long as a prophet remains among his people, he counters their excesses with this 'beautiful separation'. However, when the decision of punishment arrives from Allah, he leaves them with a declaration of disassociation, and this separation is the precursor of the nation's destruction.

A severe threat to the deniers:
Addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), this is a severe threat to his deniers: leave their matter to Me, and grant them a little more respite. The meaning is: see what end they face!
In 'And leave Me and the deniers' (wa dharnee wa-l-mukadhdhibeen), the style suggests: step aside and let Me deal with these rebels alone; you do not need to raise your hand.
This also indicates that the delay in the destruction of these deniers is because you are still among them. The divine law is that as long as a prophet remains among his people, the punishment does not befall them. Once you leave, these rebels will be devastated in the twinkling of an eye. It is the misfortune of these wrongdoers that they are trying to harm you, whereas you are the only wall of protection for them against the punishment. If they deprive themselves of this protection, who will save them from the punishment?
'Uli-n-na'mah' means possessors of ease and luxury. The word 'na'mah' is used in the sense of luxury and ease. Referencing this attribute of the deniers is intended to point to the cause behind their denial and to rebuke them for their ungratefulness. The meaning is: Allah Almighty blessed them with ease and luxury, so they should have been grateful to their Lord; but the prosperity He bestowed became a source of pride for them, and they rose as rivals to their Lord.

That is, since the prosperity We bestowed in this world became a source of pride and denial of the Messenger for them, they should remember that We have shackles and a burning Fire in the Hereafter—meaning they will be bound in shackles and chains and cast into Hell.
'Ankal' is the plural of 'nikl', which means a shackle as well as an iron bridle. In other places, 'salasil' (chains) and 'aghlal' (shackles) have appeared.

'And food that chokes and a painful punishment' (wa ta'aman dha ghussatiw-wa 'adhaban aleema) - That is: We blessed them with delicious food which they did not appreciate, so they should remember that in the Hereafter they will receive food that will choke in their throats, and they will face a painful punishment instead of ease.

This points to the Day when these arrogant individuals will face these conditions. It is stated: on that Day, the earth and mountains will shake, and let alone the palaces and castles of the wealthy, even the mountains will become like pouring sand dunes.

Warning to the Quraysh:
After giving essential instructions to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), this warns the Quraysh: just as We sent a messenger to Pharaoh, We have sent a messenger to you as a witness to inform you of Our pleasure and commands—showing that if you accept them, you will receive a great reward in this world and the Hereafter, and if you disobey, what end you will face. The meaning is: understand the status and rank of the person you are denying and mocking, and consider the consequences of denying him. He is not a beggar or a mere preacher whose acceptance or rejection holds no weight; rather, Allah has sent him as His witness over you; therefore, a decisive judgment between truth and falsehood will inevitably manifest through him, just as it manifested between Moses and Pharaoh. Just as Pharaoh disobeyed the messenger and Allah seized him—seizing him in such a manner that he found no escape—similarly He will seize you in a manner from which no one can save you.
We have explained the complete meaning of 'a witness over you' (shahidan 'alaykum) under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 143.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 15 and 16 was combined in the original text.]

That is, if you are not being seized immediately, do not assume you will never be seized. Even if you are not seized in this world, the Hereafter is bound to happen; so consider how you will protect yourselves—if you deny the Messenger—against a Day that will turn children white-haired! 'Turning children white-haired' is an idiom expressing the intensity of terror. In our language, we say: 'This shock has made me old.' Narrations also state that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) said: 'Surah Hud and its sister Surahs have made me white-haired.' Classical Arab poets have also used this idiom in various ways. Since it is well known, there is no need to reproduce poetic verses. Zamakhshari has cited reliable verses.

That is, do not regard the Day of Resurrection as something impossible. The heaven is split open by its weight; it may split open and the Resurrection may appear through it while you are in heedlessness. This theme was described in Surah Al-A'raf as:
(It is heavy in the heavens and the earth. It will not come to you except unexpectedly.) (Al-A'raf, 7:187).
That is, the Resurrection is not something that requires proof; its signs are visible in the heavens and earth just like the pregnancy of a woman in her final days. Its exact time of birth cannot be stated, but anyone with eyes knows she will give birth. Similarly, the signs of the Resurrection are visible, and the heaven is split open by its weight, such that its occurrence is expected at any moment. Unfortunate are those who are untroubled by it merely because its exact time has not been communicated to them.

'This' (hadhihi) refers to the verses of the Qur'an recited to the Quraysh to remind them of the Hereafter. The meaning is: warning and reminding were necessary before the punishment. Thus, Allah Almighty established His proof by sending His Messenger. Now, the responsibility lies on the people themselves; whoever wishes may choose the path to his Lord and become worthy of His mercy and pleasure, and whoever wishes may persist in deviation and face its end. Allah has no concern for them.

Explanation regarding some of the initial commands:
This is the final verse of the Surah. Its subject shows that it was revealed in Medina, but it relates to the command of the night prayer given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) at the beginning of the Surah; therefore, it was placed at the end of this Surah to help understand the nature of both the previous and subsequent commands and their relationship. Examples of this have appeared previously, serving as a clear proof that the arrangement of verses in the Surahs is based on their semantic relationship, and this was done under the command of Allah Almighty and the direction of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).
'Indeed, your Lord knows, [O Muhammad], that you stand [in prayer] almost two-thirds of the night or half of it or a third of it' (inna rabbaka ya'lamu annaka taqumu adna min thuluthayi-l-layli wa nisfahu wa thuluthah) - This praises the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): Allah Almighty knows well that you are observing the command of the night prayer with complete dedication. The meaning is: your efforts are in Allah's knowledge, and since they are in His knowledge, they will not go in vain; rather, you will receive a full reward.
'and [so do] a group of those with you' (wa ta'ifatum-minal-ladhina ma'ak) - Following the praise of the Prophet, this praises those Muslims who, out of desire to follow him, took this obligation upon themselves. Although the command was specifically for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the companions (may Allah be pleased with them) would hasten to follow him in every action. Although true faith in the Messenger requires following his every footstep, there is a major difference between the capacity of the Messenger and that of others; therefore, Allah Almighty altered the nature of this command for the common Muslims—as clarified further on—so that this burden would not exceed their capacity.
Mitigation for the common Muslims: 'And Allah determines the night and the day. He knows that you [all] cannot maintain it, so He has turned to you in forgiveness; so recite what is easy [for you] of the Qur'an' (wa-llahu yuqaddiru-l-layla wa-n-nahar, 'alima al-lan tuhsoohu fa-taba 'alaykum faqra'u ma tayassara minal-qur'an) - It is stated: Allah Almighty is the One Who determines the night and day. He knows well what their requirements and demands are, how they affect life, how dependent man is on them, and what situations and difficulties he must face. In the light of His knowledge, His decision is that you will be unable to maintain the night prayer for half, a third, or two-thirds of the night; so recite what is easy of the Qur'an.
The analysis of 'taba 'alaykum' (He has turned to you in forgiveness) appeared in its relevant place. When 'taba' is connected with 'ala', it carries the meaning of turning in mercy and favor.
Although the words 'so recite what is easy of the Qur'an' are general, they are mentioned in the context of the night prayer; therefore, the objective is to recite the Qur'an in Tahajjud. Although reciting the Qur'an is an act of worship in itself, reciting it in Tahajjud with measured recitation is what yields the blessings for which the Prophet was commanded in this Surah.
This shows that this mitigation was granted to the common Muslims because they could not bear this heavy burden. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself remained firm on the original command until the final moments of his life. For the common believers, this remains as a virtuous act, left to the desire and capacity of each individual.
'He knows that there will be among you those who are ill and others traveling throughout the land seeking of the bounty of Allah and others fighting in the cause of Allah. So recite what is easy from it' (alima an sayakunu minkum-marda wa akharuna yadribuna fil-ardi yabtaghuna min fadlillahi wa akharuna yuqatiloona fi sabeelillah faqra'u ma tayassara minhu).
Reasons for mitigation: This points to the situations that can arise and necessitated this mitigation. It is stated: some among you will be ill, some traveling for religious or worldly needs, and some fighting in the cause of Allah; therefore, to share in this blessing, it is sufficient to spend whatever time you can find and recite whatever portion of the Qur'an is easy for you.
'traveling throughout the land seeking of the bounty of Allah' refers to any travel undertaken for a noble purpose, whether for seeking knowledge, Hajj, or trade. Traveling for trade is described in these words in various places of the Qur'an.
'And establish prayer and give zakat and loan Allah a goodly loan' (wa aqeemu-s-salata wa atu-z-zakata wa aqridu-llaha qardan hasana) - This points to the way to compensate: if you cannot share fully in the blessings of the night prayer, maintain the five daily prayers, give zakat, and spend generously in the cause of Allah for the defense and urgent needs of the nation. In this manner, you can attain proximity to your Lord and His pleasure.
'and loan Allah a goodly loan' (wa aqridu-llaha qardan hasana) can refer to any spending in the cause of Allah with generosity, but when mentioned alongside giving zakat, it refers specifically to the spending required for war, jihad, or an urgent national need.
'And whatever good you put forward for yourselves—you will find it with Allah. It is better and greater in reward' (wa ma tuqaddimu li-anfusikum-min khayrin tajiduhu 'indallahi huwa khayraw-wa a'dhama ajra) - This encourages and inspires spending: whatever you spend in the cause of Allah is not for anyone else, but for yourselves. It will be credited to your account with God, and you will find it in an excellent and highly beneficial form with your Lord. The meaning is: this is not a losing transaction, but the most profitable trade.
'And seek forgiveness of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful' (wa-staghfiru-llah, inna-llaha ghafoorur-raheem) - That is: along with these commitments, constantly pray to your Lord for the forgiveness of your shortcomings and errors, and hope He will forgive. He is ever Forgiving and Merciful.
